subtitle: Scales Already Shattered
location_text: Dual POV. Erica Campbell in the PetroTex courtroom (left); Anna Logue in her studio across the river (right).
format_note: Justice is one of the THREE REFUSED arcana per the Priestess journal — but unlike VI Lovers (the card stays face-down) and XX Judgement (the deck refuses), this card flipped face-up anyway and split into two POVs. The refusal didn't take.

ciphers.text: the scales — both pans on the floor, the beam still in Erica's hand
ciphers.encoding_hint: the classical Justice card weighs; this card has already weighed. The shattered scales are not a failure of judgement — they are judgement's exhaust. The beam still in Erica's hand means the rendering happened; the scales were never going to survive it. Erica did her job.

ciphers.text: PETROTEX v. ROMERO et al. — file open on Erica's bench, photo paper-clipped to the cover
ciphers.encoding_hint: the case file names Mrs. Romero as a plaintiff. The photo paper-clipped to the cover is the SAME photo on Anna's desk in the studio panel — the case has two readers, both correct, neither in the same room.
cross_references: empress.json (Nicola → ANXIETY SPIKE near a courtroom date)

ciphers.text: the NARWHAL mug — Anna's, almost empty, on the studio side of the split
ciphers.encoding_hint: the mug is canon: Anna drinks from it. The narwhal is the deck's secondary-fauna companion to Faith (the dog). Aquatic, solitary, witness. Where Faith carries through the dry stations (Fool / Chariot / Strength), the narwhal carries through the water stations — and Justice is the wettest courtroom in the deck.

ciphers.text: Mrs. Romero's photo — the same image on the case file and on Anna's desk
ciphers.encoding_hint: the photo bridges the dual POV. Two rooms, one image. Two readings, one face. The classical Justice card weighs two pans; Modern Mythology's Justice weighs two POVs of the same evidence and finds them equally true. The shattered scales were never going to reconcile that.

ciphers.text: ANNA'S WIREFRAME DECONSTRUCTION — pinned above the studio drafting table
ciphers.encoding_hint: Anna's reading of the case is a wireframe — structural, predictive. Where Erica argues the case in court, Anna deconstructs it in the studio. The classical Magician's predictive model city (I) returns here as Anna's predictive wireframe. Justice's left/right split is the Empress's burgundy/emerald split rendered in document form.

ciphers.text: the bottom-right status panel: XI / JUSTICE or VIII (subtle annotation)
ciphers.encoding_hint: Modern Mythology's Justice card carries the historical Justice/Strength swap annotation — pre-1909 decks had Strength at XI and Justice at VIII. The card is subtly aware of its own renumbering. The Priestess marked Justice as refused; the Strength card stands at VIII as the gentle answer. The numbering ambiguity is the deck's acknowledgement that order itself is a judgement call.

hotspots.notes: vertical centerline — clicking flips which POV dominates the gallery view; mirrors the empress.json split_toggle. Two cards share the dual-POV mechanic; they are the only two.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has read the THREE REFUSED register on the Lovers card. A small upper-left annotation now reads 'PRIESTESS JOURNAL ENTRY: I am not the one to draw it.' Justice was refused — but Justice flipped anyway, split into two POVs, and is being read by the player. The Priestess declined the rendering; the card rendered itself. The deck's three refused cards behave differently: Lovers stays face-down, Wheel is unpainted, Justice flipped without permission.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has activated the dual_pov_node on Empress or Emperor. A small floor annotation reads 'DUAL-POV: III (Empress, sensory ↔ data) → XI (Justice, court ↔ studio). The mechanic the Empress invented at chapter III is the mechanic Justice runs at chapter XI — Nicola/Aria become Erica/Anna at structural scale.' III → XI is the dual-POV inheritance line; the deck's only two split cards.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has read the Fortress Menu on the Wheel of Fortune card. The PetroTex case file on Erica's bench now visibly shows the four Wheel files tucked inside as a single labelled stack — CORPORATE SLATE / DENIED RED / CONTACT BLUE / SETTLEMENT GOLD. The fortress's four corners are present in the courtroom as the case-file's interior. X → XI is the deck's tightest character adjacency; what Erica selected from in the office is what she now argues from at the bench.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has opened Alberto's Call Log on the Wheel. The case file on Erica's bench now visibly has 'Alberto — see X' annotated in her own hand. Alberto was named at the Wheel; Justice acknowledges the prior naming. The deck does not introduce characters at the courtroom; the courtroom remembers.

DORMANT: appears only after Antonio-as-son is recognized. Mrs. Romero's photo on Erica's bench (and on Anna's desk) now visibly carries a top-line annotation in fine pencil: 'former employer: D'Ambrosio household, 1985-2005. position: housekeeper. references: family contact A. D'Ambrosio (deceased).' Marta worked for the family for twenty years. Her PetroTex deposition was the deposition of a former family employee about the family's practices; the family moved the dates to discredit her two-decade memory. The shattered scales are the scales of a twenty-year loyalty broken by paperwork. The painted card's case file at Erica's bench is the case file of a family that does not pay what it owes its workers when the workers begin to speak. Anna's wireframe deconstruction is partly her mother's witness rendered as architecture.

DORMANT: appears only after the barge voicelog surfaces Dante's 09:02:54 confession ('I am waiting for the council to forget the date-shift'). The PetroTex case file on Erica's bench now visibly carries a small clip — a transcript fragment Erica could not have legally obtained: 'For the council to forget the date-shift.' The case Erica argues is the case Dante engineered to be forgotten. She does not have admissible access to this evidence; the deck has it; the demons have it; the case proceeds anyway. The shattered scales include the verdict that would have followed if the demons' record were the court's record.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has read the Bed 5 nurse's note in the Death (XIII) Ward C diorama. Mrs. Romero's photo on Erica's bench now visibly has a hospital admit-band tucked behind it. Marta Romero — Anna's mother, Erica's client — was in Bed 5 of Ward C the night of Walpurgisnacht. Comfortable. Family stayed until 22:00. The case the chapter argues is the case of a living plaintiff who is one ward over. Erica did not know. The PetroTex side did. They have been arguing against a deposition they knew was being given by a woman in a hospital bed.

easter_eggs: Justice is one of the three refused arcana per Priestess journal (VI Lovers, XI Justice, XX Judgement). But Justice did not stay refused — it flipped, split into two POVs, and is now legible. The refusal is itself a tellable story; Justice tells it.

the Justice / Strength numbering swap is canon in tarot historiography — pre-1909 decks had Strength at XI, Justice at VIII. This card carries that annotation as a self-aware nod to its own contested position.

the dual-POV mechanic is shared with Empress (III). The two split cards are the deck's only dual-POV chapters, and one informs the other — Empress invented the visual grammar Justice runs at structural scale.
Mrs. Romero's photo on both panels makes the card 'true twice.' Justice in this chapter is not the picking of one truth over another but the carrying of both.
the narwhal is the deck's water-fauna through-line — Faith (dog) carries dry stations, narwhal carries wet ones. Justice is the wettest courtroom in the deck.
